commands.short_description: Sets the exact pixel width of the game window. Only applies when Screen Resolution Mode is set to -1 (Custom).
commands.description: Sets the exact pixel width of the game window. Only applies when Screen Resolution Mode is set to -1 (Custom).
commands.pro_tip: Pair 3440 with -1 mode on ultrawide — the extra horizontal FOV is the secret to seeing flankers 200ms earlier than 16:9 players.

commands.name: r_customHeight
commands.short_description: Sets the exact pixel height of the game window. Only applies when Screen Resolution Mode is set to -1 (Custom).
commands.description: Sets the exact pixel height of the game window. Only applies when Screen Resolution Mode is set to -1 (Custom).
commands.pro_tip: Use 1440 on high-refresh monitors — the extra vertical pixels make rail tracking smoother without sacrificing the 250+ FPS you need for perfect movement.

commands.name: r_fullscreen
commands.short_description: Toggles between Windowed and Full Screen mode. Competitive players universally play on Full Screen to minimize Windows Desktop Window Manager (DWM) input lag.
commands.description: Toggles between Windowed and Full Screen mode. Competitive players universally play on Full Screen to minimize Windows Desktop Window Manager (DWM) input lag.
commands.pro_tip: Always use true Fullscreen (not borderless) — it cuts input latency by 8–12ms compared to windowed, which is the difference between hitting or missing that clutch rail.

commands.name: com_maxfps
commands.short_description: Caps the maximum frames per second. 250 is the gold standard for Quake Live. Even if your monitor is only 144Hz, running the game at 250 FPS dramatically reduces input latency and ensures perfectly stable strafe-jumping physics. Higher (500+) if stable >250Hz monitor. Uncapped risks stutter.
commands.description: Caps the maximum frames per second. 250 is the gold standard for Quake Live. Even if your monitor is only 144Hz, running the game at 250 FPS dramatically reduces input latency and ensures perfectly stable strafe-jumping physics. Higher (500+) if stable >250Hz monitor. Uncapped risks stutter.
commands.pro_tip: Lock at 250 even on a 144Hz monitor — the extra frames reduce input lag and make strafe-jumping timing feel buttery smooth (this is why every QPL pro runs 250+).

commands.name: r_displayRefresh
commands.short_description: Forces a specific refresh rate (Hz) from your monitor. Keeping this at 0 allows your GPU and operating system to seamlessly apply your highest desktop refresh rate. Change this if you are using custom stretched resolutions that break your Hz.
commands.description: Forces a specific refresh rate (Hz) from your monitor. Keeping this at 0 allows your GPU and operating system to seamlessly apply your highest desktop refresh rate. Change this if you are using custom stretched resolutions that break your Hz.
commands.pro_tip: Leave at 0 — your GPU will automatically use the highest Hz your monitor supports, which is exactly what every high-refresh player wants for zero tearing without V-Sync.

commands.name: r_swapInterval
commands.short_description: Locks the game's frame rate to your monitor's refresh rate to prevent screen tearing. Warning: Competitive players MUST leave this Off, as V-Sync introduces severe mouse input lag.
commands.description: Locks the game's frame rate to your monitor's refresh rate to prevent screen tearing. Warning: Competitive players MUST leave this Off, as V-Sync introduces severe mouse input lag.
commands.pro_tip: Keep this OFF forever — the 8–16ms of added mouse lag from V-Sync is the reason pros miss rails they should be hitting.

commands.name: cg_viewsize
commands.short_description: Reduces the actual rendered 3D game world viewport size while keeping the 2D HUD overlay at full size. Originally used on 1990s PCs for performance; today, this should always be left at 100.
commands.description: Reduces the actual rendered 3D game world viewport size while keeping the 2D HUD overlay at full size. Originally used on 1990s PCs for performance; today, this should always be left at 100.
commands.pro_tip: Never drop below 100 — lowering this creates black borders that hide map geometry and actually hurts visibility more than it helps performance on modern hardware.

commands.name: r_ignoreHWGamma
commands.short_description: Determines whether the game overrides monitor gamma (0) or uses desktop (1). Set to 0 for in-game gamma/overbright control—essential for custom brightness.
commands.description: Determines whether the game overrides monitor gamma (0) or uses desktop (1). Set to 0 for in-game gamma/overbright control—essential for custom brightness.
commands.pro_tip: Leave at 0 — this is the only way the in-game r_gamma and r_mapOverBrightBits actually work, which is why every pro config has it disabled.

commands.name: r_ext_gamma_control
commands.short_description: Enables direct GPU driver gamma access (NVIDIA/AMD). Keep on for reliable adjustments; off may limit control on some hardware.
commands.description: Enables direct GPU driver gamma access (NVIDIA/AMD). Keep on for reliable adjustments; off may limit control on some hardware.
commands.pro_tip: Keep this ON — it lets NVIDIA/AMD control panels override Quake gamma for perfect black levels on OLED monitors without touching in-game sliders.

commands.name: r_gamma
commands.short_description: Overall screen luminance multiplier. Higher values brighten dark areas without color shift. IPS/OLED often need 1.1-1.3; fullscreen required.
commands.description: Overall screen luminance multiplier. Higher values brighten dark areas without color shift. IPS/OLED often need 1.1-1.3; fullscreen required.
commands.pro_tip: Set to 1.2–1.3 — this is the exact range Cypher and most 2026 pros use to reveal enemies hiding in dark corners without washing out the entire screen.

commands.name: r_mapOverBrightBits
commands.short_description: Ambient map geometry brightness multiplier. Higher makes shadows drastically lighter; 2-3 balances visibility without washout.
commands.description: Ambient map geometry brightness multiplier. Higher makes shadows drastically lighter; 2-3 balances visibility without washout.
commands.pro_tip: Set to 3 on dark maps like q3dm17 — it turns deep shadows into readable areas so you can spot campers without cranking gamma to ugly levels.

commands.name: r_overBrightBits
commands.short_description: Multiplies the brightness of moving entities (players, items, weapons). If you use Vertex Lighting (r_vertexLight 1), you MUST set this to 2 or higher, otherwise enemy models will appear completely pitch black. Fullscreen required.
commands.description: Multiplies the brightness of moving entities (players, items, weapons). If you use Vertex Lighting (r_vertexLight 1), you MUST set this to 2 or higher, otherwise enemy models will appear completely pitch black. Fullscreen required.
commands.pro_tip: Set to 2 when using vertex lighting — enemies and rail slugs glow just enough to stand out against walls without looking like neon signs.

commands.name: r_intensity
commands.short_description: Acts as a saturation and vibrance multiplier. While Gamma washes the screen out with white light, Intensity specifically makes map and enemy colors 'pop' more aggressively. 1.1-1.2 enhances enemy visibility subtly.
commands.description: Acts as a saturation and vibrance multiplier. While Gamma washes the screen out with white light, Intensity specifically makes map and enemy colors 'pop' more aggressively. 1.1-1.2 enhances enemy visibility subtly.
commands.pro_tip: Set to 1.2 — this makes red armor and enemy models literally pop against the map without touching gamma, which is the cleanest visibility trick pros use.

commands.name: r_mapOverBrightCap
commands.short_description: Sets a hard limit on how bright map textures can become when using high Overbright settings. 255 allows full range without clipping—default for natural lighting.
commands.description: Sets a hard limit on how bright map textures can become when using high Overbright settings. 255 allows full range without clipping—default for natural lighting.
commands.pro_tip: Leave at 255 — anything lower clips bright areas and makes maps look muddy, while 255 keeps the full dynamic range the engine was designed for.

commands.name: r_fullbright
commands.short_description: Renders all textures on the map without any shadows or lighting. Warning: This is considered a cheat by the Quake Live engine and will NOT work in online multiplayer servers. Requires sv_cheats 1.
commands.description: Renders all textures on the map without any shadows or lighting. Warning: This is considered a cheat by the Quake Live engine and will NOT work in online multiplayer servers. Requires sv_cheats 1.
commands.pro_tip: Only use in local practice with sv_cheats 1 — perfect for learning map layouts or making clean frag videos, but never in ranked (instant ban).

commands.name: r_picmip
commands.short_description: Controls the level of detail for map textures. Increasing flattens to clay (easy spot enemies); 0 sharp. Pros usually use 5 or above to smooth out map textures in order to see enemy models easier.
commands.description: Controls the level of detail for map textures. Increasing flattens to clay (easy spot enemies); 0 sharp. Pros usually use 5 or above to smooth out map textures in order to see enemy models easier.
commands.pro_tip: Set to 12–16 like Cypher — turning the map into flat solid colors makes enemy models literally pop out like neon signs against the background, giving you a massive tracking advantage.

commands.name: r_textureMode
commands.short_description: Sets the OpenGL texture filtering method. Controls how smoothly textures blend as they recede into the distance. Texture filter: nearest pixel; linear sharp; trilinear smooth. Linear_mipmap_nearest pros for edge clarity.
commands.description: Sets the OpenGL texture filtering method. Controls how smoothly textures blend as they recede into the distance. Texture filter: nearest pixel; linear sharp; trilinear smooth. Linear_mipmap_nearest pros for edge clarity.
commands.pro_tip: Switch to GL_LINEAR_MIPMAP_NEAREST — the razor-sharp floor lines make grenade bounce prediction and rail tracking infinitely easier than the blurry default trilinear look.

available_settings.description: GL_NEAREST - Pixelated, no distance blending (Significant shimmering)
GL_LINEAR_MIPMAP_NEAREST - Bilinear (Sharp, but creates visible lines on floors)
GL_NEAREST_MIPMAP_LINEAR - Retro Pixelated (Quake 2 aesthetic with smooth distance)

commands.name: r_colorbits
commands.short_description: Sets the video color depth bits. Lowering this to 16-bit was popular in 1999 for performance, today 32 full range, no banding.
commands.description: Sets the video color depth bits. Lowering this to 16-bit was popular in 1999 for performance, today 32 full range, no banding.
commands.pro_tip: Stick with 32-bit — anything lower creates ugly color banding on rail beams and quad glows that can actually hide enemies in dark corners.

commands.name: r_texturebits
commands.short_description: Sets the texture color depth. Always leave at 32-bit for crisp display.
commands.description: Sets the texture color depth. Always leave at 32-bit for crisp display.
commands.pro_tip: Never drop below 32 — 16-bit textures create horrible dithering on player models that makes tracking fast-moving enemies noticeably harder.

commands.name: r_depthbits
commands.short_description: Defines the precision of Z-buffering (how the engine calculates depth). Keeping this at 32 prevents 'Z-fighting' where textures flicker rapidly when overlapping.
commands.description: Defines the precision of Z-buffering (how the engine calculates depth). Keeping this at 32 prevents 'Z-fighting' where textures flicker rapidly when overlapping.
commands.pro_tip: 32-bit is mandatory — lower values cause Z-fighting on ramps and rail beams that can make you miss shots by a single pixel.

commands.name: r_detailtextures
commands.short_description: Enables high-resolution detail textures (small-scale bump/grain overlays) on surfaces when viewed up close. Improves visual detail and realism on floors, walls, and objects at short range without affecting distant geometry.
commands.description: Enables high-resolution detail textures (small-scale bump/grain overlays) on surfaces when viewed up close. Improves visual detail and realism on floors, walls, and objects at short range without affecting distant geometry.
commands.pro_tip: Turn it OFF — removing the grain makes walls and floors perfectly smooth so your eye instantly locks onto moving enemies instead of getting distracted by static noise.

commands.name: r_lodBias
commands.short_description: Controls the geometric complexity of player and weapon models (Model detail threshold). Setting this to -2 ensures player models render at maximum visual size, matching their hitboxes more accurately.
commands.description: Controls the geometric complexity of player and weapon models (Model detail threshold). Setting this to -2 ensures player models render at maximum visual size, matching their hitboxes more accurately.
commands.pro_tip: Lock at -2 — models always render at full detail so their silhouette perfectly matches the cylindrical hitbox, eliminating those “why did my rail miss” moments.

commands.name: r_lodscale
commands.short_description: Controls the global level-of-detail (LOD) distance scaling for models and geometry. 10 = default (balanced detail falloff). Higher values = models drop to lower detail sooner/farther away (cheaper rendering). Lower values = higher detail retained at distance (more GPU cost). Recommended: 10–20 for competitive play.
commands.description: Controls the global level-of-detail (LOD) distance scaling for models and geometry. 10 = default (balanced detail falloff). Higher values = models drop to lower detail sooner/farther away (cheaper rendering). Lower values = higher detail retained at distance (more GPU cost). Recommended: 10–20 for competitive play.
commands.pro_tip: Leave at 5 — models stay detailed at longer ranges so you can still perfectly track a rail duel across the entire map.

commands.name: r_simpleMipMaps
commands.short_description: Enables faster, simplified mipmap computation with negligible visual impact. Pros turn on for minor FPS gains and smoother rendering on demanding maps.
commands.description: Enables faster, simplified mipmap computation with negligible visual impact. Pros turn on for minor FPS gains and smoother rendering on demanding maps.
commands.pro_tip: Keep this ON — the tiny performance gain stacks up on 32-player servers and the mipmaps look identical to the eye.

commands.name: r_stencilbits
commands.short_description: Allocates an 8-bit stencil buffer for advanced effects like dynamic shadows or decals (if supported). In modern QL, dynamic shadows are limited to simple blobs (cg_shadows 1) and do not require stencil. Pros always set to 0 to save VRAM and eliminate any shadow-related overhead.
commands.description: Allocates an 8-bit stencil buffer for advanced effects like dynamic shadows or decals (if supported). In modern QL, dynamic shadows are limited to simple blobs (cg_shadows 1) and do not require stencil. Pros always set to 0 to save VRAM and eliminate any shadow-related overhead.
commands.pro_tip: Keep at 0 — stencil buffer is unnecessary in QL since real dynamic shadows are not available. Disabling shadows (cg_shadows 0) + r_stencilbits 0 gives the cleanest visuals and best performance for tracking enemies.

commands.name: r_allowExtensions
commands.short_description: Allows use of GPU OpenGL extensions for advanced rendering (multitexture, etc.). Must be on for modern perf/features; off legacy debug.
commands.description: Allows use of GPU OpenGL extensions for advanced rendering (multitexture, etc.). Must be on for modern perf/features; off legacy debug.
commands.pro_tip: Never turn this off — it’s required for proper modern GPU performance and every single pro config has it enabled.

commands.name: r_roundImagesDown
commands.short_description: Rounds down texture dimensions to the nearest power of 2. Essential for proper OpenGL texture mapping without visual glitches.
commands.description: Rounds down texture dimensions to the nearest power of 2. Essential for proper OpenGL texture mapping without visual glitches.
commands.pro_tip: Leave ON — this prevents the random texture shimmering and stretching that can make rail shots look like they’re hitting invisible walls.

commands.name: r_facePlaneCull
commands.short_description: Allows the engine to skip rendering brush faces that are completely obscured. Essential for proper engine rendering.
commands.description: Allows the engine to skip rendering brush faces that are completely obscured. Essential for proper engine rendering.
commands.pro_tip: Keep ON — it silently saves FPS on every map by not drawing walls you can’t see, stacking up to huge gains in 32-player games.

commands.name: r_ext_compressed_textures
commands.short_description: Compresses texture files to save VRAM. Should always be left Off, as compression can introduce ugly visual artifacts.
commands.description: Compresses texture files to save VRAM. Should always be left Off, as compression can introduce ugly visual artifacts.
commands.pro_tip: Leave OFF — compressed textures create ugly blocky artifacts on rail beams and player skins that actually make enemies harder to see.

commands.name: r_ext_multitexture
commands.short_description: Enables the GPU to render multiple textures in a single pass. Vital for proper modern OpenGL performance.
commands.description: Enables the GPU to render multiple textures in a single pass. Vital for proper modern OpenGL performance.
commands.pro_tip: Must stay ON — this is what lets modern GPUs run Quake Live at 500+ FPS without breaking a sweat.

commands.name: r_ext_compiled_vertex_array
commands.short_description: Allows the engine to send batched geometry data to the GPU memory. Must remain On for proper game rendering.
commands.description: Allows the engine to send batched geometry data to the GPU memory. Must remain On for proper game rendering.
commands.pro_tip: Keep ON — batched geometry is the reason the game runs smoothly even on 10-year-old GPUs with 32 players on screen.

commands.name: r_ext_texture_env_add
commands.short_description: Enables additive blending in OpenGL instead of restrictive multiplicative blending. Required for proper lighting effects.
commands.description: Enables additive blending in OpenGL instead of restrictive multiplicative blending. Required for proper lighting effects.
commands.pro_tip: Leave ON — this is what makes rail beams and quad glows look correct; turning it off breaks half the visual feedback in the game.

commands.name: r_enablePostProcess
commands.short_description: Master toggle for GPU post-processing effects like Bloom (glow), Color Correction (tinting/saturation), and minor shaders. Vignette (cg_vignette) works independently. Competitive players universally turn this Off to eliminate blinding glows, unpredictable color shifts, and FPS drops during heavy combat.
commands.description: Master toggle for GPU post-processing effects like Bloom (glow), Color Correction (tinting/saturation), and minor shaders. Vignette (cg_vignette) works independently. Competitive players universally turn this Off to eliminate blinding glows, unpredictable color shifts, and FPS drops during heavy combat.
commands.pro_tip: Turn this OFF immediately — every single QPL finalist in 2026 runs without post-processing because the extra glow and color grading hide rail trails and make enemies blend into explosions.

commands.name: r_enableColorCorrect
commands.short_description: Applies a cinematic color grading filter over the screen for stylized tones. Disable for raw, accurate colors that preserve enemy visibility and map contrast without artificial tints.
commands.description: Applies a cinematic color grading filter over the screen for stylized tones. Disable for raw, accurate colors that preserve enemy visibility and map contrast without artificial tints.
commands.pro_tip: Disable it — the raw unfiltered colors make red armor and enemy skins stand out like beacons against any map, which is why no serious player uses color grading.

commands.name: cg_vignette
commands.short_description: Applies a darkened radial shadow around the screen edges for 'cinematic depth'. Off maximizes peripheral brightness for spotting flanking enemies or powerups.
commands.description: Applies a darkened radial shadow around the screen edges for 'cinematic depth'. Off maximizes peripheral brightness for spotting flanking enemies or powerups.
commands.pro_tip: Keep it OFF — the darkened edges hide flankers and powerups in your peripheral vision, costing you precious reaction time in every duel.

commands.name: r_enableBloom
commands.short_description: Creates a cinematic 'glow' effect around bright objects, explosions, rail trails, and lights. Off ensures sharp, readable projectiles and no distracting blur in fast fights.
commands.description: Creates a cinematic 'glow' effect around bright objects, explosions, rail trails, and lights. Off ensures sharp, readable projectiles and no distracting blur in fast fights.
commands.pro_tip: Turn it OFF — bloom glow completely hides incoming rail trails and rocket paths, which is why you never see it in any top player’s config.

commands.name: r_bloomBrightThreshhold
commands.short_description: Sets the luminance cutoff for bloom glow—low triggers widespread blur; high limits to extreme whites only. Higher values reduce over-glow for cleaner visuals.
commands.description: Sets the luminance cutoff for bloom glow—low triggers widespread blur; high limits to extreme whites only. Higher values reduce over-glow for cleaner visuals.
commands.pro_tip: Raise to 0.75 when you must have bloom — only the brightest explosions glow, keeping rail trails and player outlines razor sharp.

commands.name: r_bloomIntensity
commands.short_description: Controls the strength and spread of bloom glow. Low keeps it subtle; high creates overwhelming halos that obscure details during explosions or rail trails.
commands.description: Controls the strength and spread of bloom glow. Low keeps it subtle; high creates overwhelming halos that obscure details during explosions or rail trails.
commands.pro_tip: Drop to 0.25 — the subtle glow still looks pretty in frag videos but doesn’t turn every rocket into a blinding flash that hides the next shot.

commands.name: r_bloomSaturation
commands.short_description: Adjusts bloom glow color vibrancy—0.0 grayscale wash; higher adds neon intensity. Low prevents colorful distractions on bright maps.
commands.description: Adjusts bloom glow color vibrancy—0.0 grayscale wash; higher adds neon intensity. Low prevents colorful distractions on bright maps.
commands.pro_tip: Set to 0.0 — monochrome bloom removes all color distractions so you only see the shape of explosions instead of neon rainbows blocking your view.

commands.name: r_bloomSceneIntensity
commands.short_description: Scales brightness of the base scene beneath bloom layers. Lower dims non-glow areas for contrast; higher evens overall exposure.
commands.description: Scales brightness of the base scene beneath bloom layers. Lower dims non-glow areas for contrast; higher evens overall exposure.
commands.pro_tip: Drop to 0.8 — the dimmer base scene makes bloom glow stand out less and keeps the overall picture crisp instead of washed out.

commands.name: r_bloomSceneSaturation
commands.short_description: Sets color vibrancy of the scene under bloom. 0.0 monochrome map with glowing highlights; higher oversaturates for vivid but cluttered look.
commands.description: Sets color vibrancy of the scene under bloom. 0.0 monochrome map with glowing highlights; higher oversaturates for vivid but cluttered look.
commands.pro_tip: Set to 0.0 — turning the entire map black-and-white with only bloom glowing makes rail slugs and quad auras impossible to miss.

commands.name: r_bloomPasses
commands.short_description: Number of bloom blur filter iterations—more passes soften glow but hit FPS. Single pass sharpest for competitive clarity.
commands.description: Number of bloom blur filter iterations—more passes soften glow but hit FPS. Single pass sharpest for competitive clarity.
commands.pro_tip: Lock at 1 pass — more passes create soft blurry glow that hides projectiles; single pass keeps everything razor sharp with zero extra GPU cost.

commands.name: r_vertexLight
commands.short_description: Replaces baked lightmaps/shadows with flat vertex lighting for even illumination. On eliminates hiding spots in shadows, revealing enemies consistently.
commands.description: Replaces baked lightmaps/shadows with flat vertex lighting for even illumination. On eliminates hiding spots in shadows, revealing enemies consistently.
commands.pro_tip: Turn it ON and set r_overBrightBits 2 — flat lighting removes every shadow hiding spot so you can see enemies no matter where they crouch.

commands.name: r_lightmap
commands.short_description: Developer mode rendering lightmaps only (black/white shadows, no textures). Off for normal play; on visualizes lighting for map analysis.
commands.description: Developer mode rendering lightmaps only (black/white shadows, no textures). Off for normal play; on visualizes lighting for map analysis.
commands.pro_tip: Only use in local practice — the black/white view instantly shows you every shadow pocket where enemies hide so you can pre-aim them in real games.

commands.name: r_dynamicLight
commands.short_description: Enables real-time light casting from rockets/plasma on walls/floors. Off prevents flashing distractions and saves GPU for stable FPS.
commands.description: Enables real-time light casting from rockets/plasma on walls/floors. Off prevents flashing distractions and saves GPU for stable FPS.
commands.pro_tip: Turn it OFF — rocket and plasma flashes no longer blind you mid-fight, giving you a huge edge in close-range plasma duels.

commands.name: cg_shadows
commands.short_description: Controls the shadow drawn underneath players. 0 none (cleanest/FPS); 1 simple blob (distance cue).
commands.description: Controls the shadow drawn underneath players. 0 none (cleanest/FPS); 1 simple blob (distance cue).
commands.pro_tip: Set to 0 — removing all player shadows eliminates the visual clutter that makes it harder to track fast-moving enemies in close quarters.

commands.name: r_ambientScale
commands.short_description: Intensity of wraparound ambient light on objects. Higher reduces deep shadows for better visibility in corners without full washout.
commands.description: Intensity of wraparound ambient light on objects. Higher reduces deep shadows for better visibility in corners without full washout.
commands.pro_tip: Bump to 15 — the extra wraparound light fills in dark corners so you can spot campers without cranking fullbright or vertex lighting.

commands.name: r_fastSky
commands.short_description: Replaces animated sky/clouds/portals with solid flat color. On eliminates motion distractions and boosts FPS on sky-heavy maps.
commands.description: Replaces animated sky/clouds/portals with solid flat color. On eliminates motion distractions and boosts FPS on sky-heavy maps.
commands.pro_tip: Turn it ON on maps like q3dm6 — the solid color sky removes all moving distractions so your eyes stay locked on enemies instead of drifting to the clouds.

commands.name: r_drawSun
commands.short_description: Renders sun lens flare/glare in skyboxes. Off prevents blinding flashes that obscure distant enemies.
commands.description: Renders sun lens flare/glare in skyboxes. Off prevents blinding flashes that obscure distant enemies.
commands.pro_tip: Keep it OFF — the sun flare can completely blind you when rotating through certain angles, costing you free kills on maps with bright skies.

commands.name: r_flares
commands.short_description: Adds a 'glare' or 'halo' effect to point-light sources and glowing projectiles. Off keeps rockets/plasma as clean spheres for precise tracking.
commands.description: Adds a 'glare' or 'halo' effect to point-light sources and glowing projectiles. Off keeps rockets/plasma as clean spheres for precise tracking.
commands.pro_tip: Turn it OFF — lens flares turn every plasma ball into a giant glowing orb that hides the actual projectile path.

commands.name: r_flareSize
commands.short_description: Diameter of lens flare halos in pixels. Larger more prominent; smaller subtler distractions.
commands.description: Diameter of lens flare halos in pixels. Larger more prominent; smaller subtler distractions.
commands.pro_tip: If you must keep flares, drop to 20 — tiny halos are almost invisible while still giving that cinematic look for videos.

commands.name: r_flareFade
commands.short_description: Distance at which flares fully dissipate. Shorter quicker cleanup; longer persistent glow.
commands.description: Distance at which flares fully dissipate. Shorter quicker cleanup; longer persistent glow.
commands.pro_tip: Set to 5 — flares disappear almost instantly so they never linger and block your view after an explosion.

commands.name: cg_waterWarp
commands.short_description: Enables the wavy visual distortion when submerged in water. This makes aiming underwater nearly impossible. Off provides clear aim for submerged fights/teleports.
commands.description: Enables the wavy visual distortion when submerged in water. This makes aiming underwater nearly impossible. Off provides clear aim for submerged fights/teleports.
commands.pro_tip: Turn it OFF — the wavy distortion makes underwater rail shots impossible to line up; pros always disable it for perfect aim in water teleports.

commands.name: cg_enableBreath
commands.short_description: Draws white puff animations coming from player models on cold maps. Off preserves clean silhouettes without particle noise.
commands.description: Draws white puff animations coming from player models on cold maps. Off preserves clean silhouettes without particle noise.
commands.pro_tip: Turn it OFF — the constant breath puffs create extra particle noise that can hide tiny movements of enemies in snowy maps.

commands.name: r_teleporterflash
commands.short_description: Flashes the screen blindingly white when you take a teleporter. Off allows instant re-aim without disorientation.
commands.description: Flashes the screen blindingly white when you take a teleporter. Off allows instant re-aim without disorientation.
commands.pro_tip: Turn it OFF — the blinding white flash after every teleport lets enemies pre-aim your exit point while you’re still re-orienting.

commands.name: r_ingamevideo
commands.short_description: Renders live video feeds on map screens (e.g., jumbotrons). Off replaces with static images for FPS/perf gains.
commands.description: Renders live video feeds on map screens (e.g., jumbotrons). Off replaces with static images for FPS/perf gains.
commands.pro_tip: Turn it OFF on maps with jumbotrons — the moving video feeds are pure distraction and cost FPS while giving zero gameplay advantage.

commands.name: cg_drawGun
commands.short_description: Sets the visibility of the weapon you are holding. 0 hidden max screen; 1 full model; 2 muzzle flash only (feedback); 3 ghost. 2 pro standard for cue without bulk.
commands.description: Sets the visibility of the weapon you are holding. 0 hidden max screen; 1 full model; 2 muzzle flash only (feedback); 3 ghost. 2 pro standard for cue without bulk.
commands.pro_tip: Set to 2 (muzzle flash only) — you get instant visual feedback on every shot without any weapon model ever blocking your crosshair, which is the exact setup used by every top-tier rail god.

commands.name: cg_gunX
commands.short_description: Moves the weapon forward or backward. Positive extends pointer; negative pulls close. 3-5 aids flick aim without obscure.
commands.description: Moves the weapon forward or backward. Positive extends pointer; negative pulls close. 3-5 aids flick aim without obscure.
commands.pro_tip: Push to +3–5 like Cypher — the extended “pointer” effect makes flicks feel more natural and keeps the rail perfectly centered on target at all times.

commands.name: cg_gunY
commands.short_description: Moves the weapon left or right. Positive values move it right; negative values move it left. Setting this to 0 places the gun directly in the center of the screen (Doom style).
commands.description: Moves the weapon left or right. Positive values move it right; negative values move it left. Setting this to 0 places the gun directly in the center of the screen (Doom style).
commands.pro_tip: Set to -7 or 0 for Doom-style centering — this is the cleanest view pros use so the weapon model never interferes with tracking or flick shots.

commands.name: cg_gunZ
commands.short_description: Moves the weapon up or down. Negative lowers bottom-clearing center vision. -7--10 pro for unobstructed crosshair.
commands.description: Moves the weapon up or down. Negative lowers bottom-clearing center vision. -7--10 pro for unobstructed crosshair.
commands.pro_tip: Drop to -7 or -8 — burying the gun low clears the entire lower half of your screen so your crosshair is never obstructed during intense duels.

commands.name: cg_railStyle
commands.short_description: Sets the visual style of the Railgun trail. The vast majority of competitive players use 'Core Only' (1), as the classic spiral (2) creates massive visual clutter and can block your view of the enemy during a fight.
commands.description: Sets the visual style of the Railgun trail. The vast majority of competitive players use 'Core Only' (1), as the classic spiral (2) creates massive visual clutter and can block your view of the enemy during a fight.
commands.pro_tip: Set to 1 (core only) — the clean single-line beam is the reason pros can land perfect rails through chaos without the spiral ever blocking their target.

commands.name: color1
commands.short_description: Sets the color of the inner railgun beam. Bright, unnatural colors like Cyan (13), Green (9), or Pink (23) are highly recommended to provide maximum contrast against dark/brown map textures.
commands.description: Sets the color of the inner railgun beam. Bright, unnatural colors like Cyan (13), Green (9), or Pink (23) are highly recommended to provide maximum contrast against dark/brown map textures.
commands.pro_tip: Use Cyan (13) or Pink (23) — these high-contrast colors make the rail beam impossible to lose against any map background, which is why Clawz and most rail gods swear by them.

commands.name: color2
commands.short_description: Sets the color of the outer railgun spiral. (Note: Only visible if Beam Style is set to 2).
commands.description: Sets the color of the outer railgun spiral. (Note: Only visible if Beam Style is set to 2).
commands.pro_tip: If you run spiral style, pair it with White (25) — the clean outer glow makes the entire beam pop without clashing with your core color.

commands.name: r_railCoreWidth
commands.short_description: Sets the physical thickness (in pixels) of the solid inner beam. A thinner beam (like 2 or 3) is highly recommended for accuracy, as thick beams can completely obscure a distant opponent.
commands.description: Sets the physical thickness (in pixels) of the solid inner beam. A thinner beam (like 2 or 3) is highly recommended for accuracy, as thick beams can completely obscure a distant opponent.
commands.pro_tip: Drop to 2–3 — the ultra-thin core is the reason pros can snipe through tiny gaps and never have the beam itself block the enemy.

commands.name: r_railWidth
commands.short_description: Sets the thickness (in pixels) of the translucent 'glow' that surrounds the solid core. Pros often reduce this to 0 to make the beam perfectly sharp and distraction-free.
commands.description: Sets the thickness (in pixels) of the translucent 'glow' that surrounds the solid core. Pros often reduce this to 0 to make the beam perfectly sharp and distraction-free.
commands.pro_tip: Set to 0 — removing the outer glow makes the rail beam razor sharp so you can perfectly read the exact impact point even at long range.

commands.name: cg_railTrailTime
commands.short_description: The time (in milliseconds) that rail trails linger in the air. Lowering this clears the screen faster, but 1500ms acts as a perfectly synchronized visual metronome (the exact time it takes for the Railgun to reload).
commands.description: The time (in milliseconds) that rail trails linger in the air. Lowering this clears the screen faster, but 1500ms acts as a perfectly synchronized visual metronome (the exact time it takes for the Railgun to reload).
commands.pro_tip: Set to 1500ms — the trail lasts exactly as long as the railgun reload, turning every shot into a perfect visual timer so you know when you’re ready to fire again without looking at the weapon icon.

commands.name: cg_railRingWidth
commands.short_description: Sets how wide the spirals loop around the core. Lower values create a tighter, more focused spiral.
commands.description: Sets how wide the spirals loop around the core. Lower values create a tighter, more focused spiral.
commands.pro_tip: If you use spiral style, drop to 8 — the tight coil looks clean and never bloats the beam so you can still see the enemy clearly.

commands.name: r_railSegmentLength
commands.short_description: Sets the polygon length of the spiral segments. Lowering this makes the spiral look like a detailed coil, while increasing it stretches the spirals into long ribbons.
commands.description: Sets the polygon length of the spiral segments. Lowering this makes the spiral look like a detailed coil, while increasing it stretches the spirals into long ribbons.
commands.pro_tip: Set to 16 for spiral style — the tight detailed coil looks premium and doesn’t create visual noise that could hide a distant target.

commands.name: cg_lightningStyle
commands.short_description: Sets the visual style of the Lightning Gun shaft. Style 4 (Thin Shaft) and 5 (Wide Beam) are the most popular competitively. Style 4 is highly recommended as it prevents the beam from obscuring the enemy's feet while tracking.
commands.description: Sets the visual style of the Lightning Gun shaft. Style 4 (Thin Shaft) and 5 (Wide Beam) are the most popular competitively. Style 4 is highly recommended as it prevents the beam from obscuring the enemy's feet while tracking.
commands.pro_tip: Style 4 (Thin Shaft) is what almost every pro and high-level player runs in 2026. The much thinner beam barely covers the enemy’s model — especially their feet and legs — so you can track movement and strafe direction way more cleanly than with the chunky default style 1.

commands.name: cg_trueLightning
commands.short_description: Controls how straight and rigid the Lightning Gun beam appears. 1.0 draws a perfectly straight line directly from your crosshair (acting as a second pointer for tracking). Values below 1.0 add a swaying delay that makes precise aiming harder.
commands.description: Controls how straight and rigid the Lightning Gun beam appears. 1.0 draws a perfectly straight line directly from your crosshair (acting as a second pointer for tracking). Values below 1.0 add a swaying delay that makes precise aiming harder.
commands.pro_tip: Lock it at 1.0 — the perfectly straight beam turns your LG into a literal laser pointer so you can track strafing enemies with zero guesswork, which is why every LG god in 2026 runs this exact value.

commands.name: cg_lightningImpact
commands.short_description: Enables the bright glowing impact effect at the point where the Lightning Gun beam hits a surface or player. Most pros keep this On for instant visual hit confirmation while tracking, but turn it Off if they find the flash too distracting.
commands.description: Enables the bright glowing impact effect at the point where the Lightning Gun beam hits a surface or player. Most pros keep this On for instant visual hit confirmation while tracking, but turn it Off if they find the flash too distracting.
commands.pro_tip: Keep it ON — the bright white impact dot is the fastest possible hit confirmation in the game, letting you instantly know when your LG is connecting even through heavy particle spam.

commands.name: cg_lightningImpactCap
commands.short_description: Controls the radius/size of the bright glowing 'cap' (white disc/flash) that appears at the exact point where the Lightning Gun beam hits a surface or player X units away. This effect only appears when cg_lightningImpact is enabled (set to 1). Higher values create a much larger and brighter flash, which can be blinding in close-range fights. Default is 192.
commands.description: Controls the radius/size of the bright glowing 'cap' (white disc/flash) that appears at the exact point where the Lightning Gun beam hits a surface or player X units away. This effect only appears when cg_lightningImpact is enabled (set to 1). Higher values create a much larger and brighter flash, which can be blinding in close-range fights. Default is 192.
commands.pro_tip: Set to 0 or 64 — removing the giant white core flash means you never get blinded when chaining LG on a close target, giving you a huge edge in plasma/LG duels.

commands.name: cg_muzzleFlash
commands.short_description: Displays a bright flash at the muzzle of your weapon when firing. Most competitive players disable this to eliminate self-induced visual distraction and keep their crosshair area as clean as possible.
commands.description: Displays a bright flash at the muzzle of your weapon when firing. Most competitive players disable this to eliminate self-induced visual distraction and keep their crosshair area as clean as possible.
commands.pro_tip: Turn it OFF — the self-flash from your own gun is the #1 reason pros miss follow-up shots during rocket or LG spam; removing it keeps your crosshair perfectly clear.

commands.name: cg_impactSparks
commands.short_description: Emits physical sparks from enemies and walls when hit by hitscan weapons. Pros often turn this Off to reduce screen clutter, relying instead on audio cues and blood for hit confirmation.
commands.description: Emits physical sparks from enemies and walls when hit by hitscan weapons. Pros often turn this Off to reduce screen clutter, relying instead on audio cues and blood for hit confirmation.
commands.pro_tip: Turn it OFF — removing spark particles clears your view so you can track the enemy through your own shots instead of staring at flying debris.

commands.name: cg_impactSparksLifetime
commands.short_description: How long (in milliseconds) impact sparks remain visible before fading. Low values provide instant visual feedback without lingering debris that can clutter the screen during fights.
commands.description: How long (in milliseconds) impact sparks remain visible before fading. Low values provide instant visual feedback without lingering debris that can clutter the screen during fights.
commands.pro_tip: Drop to 250ms — the sparks vanish instantly so they never linger and block your view during sustained hitscan fights.

commands.name: cg_impactSparksVelocity
commands.short_description: Controls how fast and far impact sparks fly outward from the hit point (can also set to negative values, and sparks will fly downward). Setting to 0  makes sparks hang statically in the air, while higher values create more dynamic particle movement.
commands.description: Controls how fast and far impact sparks fly outward from the hit point (can also set to negative values, and sparks will fly downward). Setting to 0  makes sparks hang statically in the air, while higher values create more dynamic particle movement.
commands.pro_tip: Set to 0 — static sparks never fly into your crosshair and create zero extra motion that could distract you during tracking.

commands.name: cg_damagePlum
commands.short_description: Space-separated list of weapons that show floating damage numbers when you hit an enemy. Use weapon abbreviations (g = gauntlet, mg = machinegun, sg = shotgun, etc.). Leave empty to disable completely.
commands.description: Space-separated list of weapons that show floating damage numbers when you hit an enemy. Use weapon abbreviations (g = gauntlet, mg = machinegun, sg = shotgun, etc.). Leave empty to disable completely.
commands.pro_tip: Use 'sg gl rl pg bfg' (Splash / Variable Damage Only). Constant plums on hitscan weapons like MG/LG/RG just create clutter. But seeing exact splash damage on rockets/grenades (15 vs 100) tells you instantly whether to push or disengage — this is why most pros keep only the variable-damage weapons enabled.
commands.default_value: g mg sg gl rl lg rg pg bfg gh cg ng pl hmg

available_settings.value: g mg sg gl rl lg rg pg bfg gh cg ng pl hmg

commands.name: cg_damagePlumColorStyle
commands.short_description: Changes how the floating damage numbers (plums) are colored. Style 1 is a simple white-to-red fade, Style 2 uses tiered damage colors, and Style 3 colors each plum based on the weapon that hit.
commands.description: Changes how the floating damage numbers (plums) are colored. Style 1 is a simple white-to-red fade, Style 2 uses tiered damage colors, and Style 3 colors each plum based on the weapon that hit.
commands.pro_tip: Use style 2 (Tiered Damage Colors). A bright red plum instantly tells you 'that was a fat rail/rocket hit' while a blue plum means weak hitscan tickle. This is what most competitive players run because it gives you real tactical information at a glance.

available_settings.description: 2 - Tiered Damage Colors (≤25 blue, 26-50 yellow, 51-75 orange, 76+ red)

commands.name: cg_blood
commands.short_description: Toggles blood splatter effects when players are hit. Most pros keep this On because the visible spray from behind cover provides crucial confirmation when landing blind rockets or grenades.
commands.description: Toggles blood splatter effects when players are hit. Most pros keep this On because the visible spray from behind cover provides crucial confirmation when landing blind rockets or grenades.
commands.pro_tip: Leave it ON — the blood spray you see coming from behind cover is the only way to confirm blind rocket or grenade hits without line of sight.

commands.name: cg_marks
commands.short_description: Enables bullet holes and scorch marks on walls and floors. Keeping this On gives useful tactical information about recent fights, while Off slightly improves performance.
commands.description: Enables bullet holes and scorch marks on walls and floors. Keeping this On gives useful tactical information about recent fights, while Off slightly improves performance.
commands.pro_tip: Leave it ON — the fresh bullet holes and scorch marks tell you exactly where enemies were just firing from, giving you free pre-aim information on every map.

commands.name: cg_impactMarkTime
commands.short_description: How long (in milliseconds) bullet holes and scorch marks remain visible on surfaces. 8000–15000 ms provides good tactical feedback about recent engagements without excessive clutter.
commands.description: How long (in milliseconds) bullet holes and scorch marks remain visible on surfaces. 8000–15000 ms provides good tactical feedback about recent engagements without excessive clutter.
commands.pro_tip: Set to 15000ms — the marks stay visible long enough to tell you exactly where the enemy was camping 10 seconds ago, giving you perfect pre-aim intel.

commands.name: cg_smokeRadius_RL
commands.short_description: Controls the trail radius of smoke plumes from rockets. Setting to 0 removes the smoke trail entirely.
commands.description: Controls the trail radius of smoke plumes from rockets. Setting to 0 removes the smoke trail entirely.
commands.pro_tip: Set to 0 — removing rocket smoke means your own shots never blind you or hide the enemy you’re trying to finish, which is why every RL specialist runs zero smoke.

commands.name: cg_smokeRadius_GL
commands.short_description: Controls the trail radius of smoke plumes from grenades. Setting to 0 removes lingering smoke that can blind you or obscure enemies during grenade spam.
commands.description: Controls the trail radius of smoke plumes from grenades. Setting to 0 removes lingering smoke that can blind you or obscure enemies during grenade spam.
commands.pro_tip: Set to 0 — grenade spam no longer creates a smoke wall that hides your follow-up shots or the enemy trying to escape.

commands.name: cg_smokeRadius_NG
commands.short_description: Controls the trail radius of smoke plumes from nailguns. Setting to 0 removes lingering smoke that can obscure vision during sustained fire.
commands.description: Controls the trail radius of smoke plumes from nailguns. Setting to 0 removes lingering smoke that can obscure vision during sustained fire.
commands.pro_tip: Set to 0 — the nailgun no longer creates a smoke screen that blocks your view of the enemy you’re trying to track.

commands.name: cg_smoke_SG
commands.short_description: Enables smoke puffs at the muzzle when firing the shotgun. Most pros turn this Off to reduce visual noise around their crosshair while tracking.
commands.description: Enables smoke puffs at the muzzle when firing the shotgun. Most pros turn this Off to reduce visual noise around their crosshair while tracking.
commands.pro_tip: Turn it OFF — the muzzle smoke from your shotgun no longer blocks your crosshair when you’re trying to finish someone at close range.

commands.name: cg_smokeRadius_dust
commands.short_description: Controls the size of dust/smoke plumes kicked up from the ground and map surfaces. Setting to 0 removes unnecessary particle clutter.
commands.description: Controls the size of dust/smoke plumes kicked up from the ground and map surfaces. Setting to 0 removes unnecessary particle clutter.
commands.pro_tip: Set to 0 — no more random dust clouds kicking up when you run or jump, keeping your screen perfectly clean for tracking.

commands.name: cg_smokeRadius_flight
commands.short_description: Controls the trail smoke left by players using the Flight powerup. Setting to 0 removes the trail for cleaner tracking of flying enemies.
commands.description: Controls the trail smoke left by players using the Flight powerup. Setting to 0 removes the trail for cleaner tracking of flying enemies.
commands.pro_tip: Set to 0 — flying enemies leave no trail so you can perfectly track them through the air without smoke getting in the way.

commands.name: cg_smokeRadius_haste
commands.short_description: Controls the trail smoke left by players using the Haste powerup. Setting to 0 removes the trail for cleaner tracking of hasted enemies.
commands.description: Controls the trail smoke left by players using the Haste powerup. Setting to 0 removes the trail for cleaner tracking of hasted enemies.
commands.pro_tip: Set to 0 — hasted enemies no longer leave a smoke trail that gives away their exact movement path.

commands.name: cg_plasmaStyle
commands.short_description: Controls the visual trail of plasma projectiles. Style 1 (No Trail) is the competitive standard because trails create a blinding wall of sparks that blocks your view of enemies.
commands.description: Controls the visual trail of plasma projectiles. Style 1 (No Trail) is the competitive standard because trails create a blinding wall of sparks that blocks your view of enemies.
commands.pro_tip: Set to 1 (no trail) — your own plasma no longer creates a wall of sparks that blocks your view of the enemy you’re trying to finish.

commands.name: cg_rocketStyle
commands.short_description: Controls the rocket exhaust trail. Style 1 removes the fire plume, making the rocket much thinner and easier to see past when tracking enemies through your own shots.
commands.description: Controls the rocket exhaust trail. Style 1 removes the fire plume, making the rocket much thinner and easier to see past when tracking enemies through your own shots.
commands.pro_tip: Set to 1 — removing the giant fire plume means you can perfectly see the enemy you’re aiming at even when firing rockets directly at them.

commands.name: cg_trueShotgun
commands.short_description: Forces the client to render the server’s exact, predictable shotgun pellet pattern instead of a fake randomized spread. On is the competitive standard for accurate visual feedback.
commands.description: Forces the client to render the server’s exact, predictable shotgun pellet pattern instead of a fake randomized spread. On is the competitive standard for accurate visual feedback.
commands.pro_tip: Turn it ON — the true server-accurate pellet pattern lets you instantly see exactly where your shotgun pellets landed, turning close-range tracking into pure muscle memory.

commands.name: cg_brassTime
commands.short_description: How long (in milliseconds) ejected shell casings from the Machine Gun and Shotgun remain visible and bounce on the floor. Most pros set this to 0 to eliminate distracting moving objects in peripheral vision.
commands.description: How long (in milliseconds) ejected shell casings from the Machine Gun and Shotgun remain visible and bounce on the floor. Most pros set this to 0 to eliminate distracting moving objects in peripheral vision.
commands.pro_tip: Set to 0 — bouncing shell casings are pure visual noise that can pull your eye away from the enemy at the worst possible moment.

commands.name: cg_switchOnEmpty
commands.short_description: Automatically switches to your next weapon with ammo when you attempt to fire an empty one. Most pros keep this On for smooth combat flow.
commands.description: Automatically switches to your next weapon with ammo when you attempt to fire an empty one. Most pros keep this On for smooth combat flow.
commands.pro_tip: Leave it ON — you never get stuck clicking on an empty gun mid-fight, giving you seamless flow when you run dry on your rail.

commands.name: cg_switchToEmpty
commands.short_description: Allows you to manually select a weapon even when it has zero ammo. Useful for practicing combos or weapon switching drills without ammo restrictions.
commands.description: Allows you to manually select a weapon even when it has zero ammo. Useful for practicing combos or weapon switching drills without ammo restrictions.
commands.pro_tip: Leave it ON — you can practice perfect weapon switch binds even when you’re completely out of ammo, which is how pros drill their muscle memory.

commands.name: cg_itemFX
commands.short_description: Controls spawn pad animations on items (0 = static, 1 = bounce, 2 = rotate, 3 = bounce + rotate, 4 - 7 = add low together). Setting to 0 makes items completely static, removing distracting movement that can pull your eye away from enemies.
commands.description: Controls spawn pad animations on items (0 = static, 1 = bounce, 2 = rotate, 3 = bounce + rotate, 4 - 7 = add low together). Setting to 0 makes items completely static, removing distracting movement that can pull your eye away from enemies.
commands.pro_tip: Set to 0 — static items never move or glow, so nothing pulls your eye away from the enemy you’re actually fighting.

commands.name: cg_screenDamage
commands.short_description: Controls the red tint overlay that flashes when you take damage. Setting this to fully transparent (0x00000000) prevents your screen from turning red during fights, preserving maximum visibility.
commands.description: Controls the red tint overlay that flashes when you take damage. Setting this to fully transparent (0x00000000) prevents your screen from turning red during fights, preserving maximum visibility.
commands.pro_tip: Set to fully transparent (0x00000000) — the red flash that normally blinds you when you take damage is completely removed, giving you perfect vision even at 1 HP.

commands.name: cg_screenDamage_Team
commands.short_description: Controls the screen tint when you take friendly fire. Setting this to fully transparent (0x00000000) prevents any visual distraction from teammate damage.
commands.description: Controls the screen tint when you take friendly fire. Setting this to fully transparent (0x00000000) prevents any visual distraction from teammate damage.
commands.pro_tip: Set to fully transparent — friendly fire flashes no longer blind you when a teammate accidentally shoots you during a teamfight.

commands.name: cg_bubbleTrail
commands.short_description: Draws bubble trails behind projectiles that enter water. Setting this to Off greatly improves visibility and target tracking while underwater or near water surfaces.
commands.description: Draws bubble trails behind projectiles that enter water. Setting this to Off greatly improves visibility and target tracking while underwater or near water surfaces.
commands.pro_tip: Turn it OFF — underwater projectiles no longer leave bubble trails that give away their exact path, letting you track and dodge perfectly.

commands.name: rate
commands.short_description: Maximum client bandwidth allocation in bytes per second for receiving server updates. Most players these days have great internet connections so leaving at default is recommended.
commands.description: Maximum client bandwidth allocation in bytes per second for receiving server updates. Most players these days have great internet connections so leaving at default is recommended.
commands.pro_tip: The max rate in Quake Live in 2026 is 25000, which is set by default. This is more than enough for any modern internet connection to receive all server updates without loss, so it’s best to just leave this at 25000.

commands.name: cl_maxpackets
commands.short_description: Maximum number of network packets sent to the server per second. 125 is the engine maximum and ensures the server receives your full input stream (movement, shots, jumps) every frame for precise registration. Lower values reduce accuracy during fast play.
commands.description: Maximum number of network packets sent to the server per second. 125 is the engine maximum and ensures the server receives your full input stream (movement, shots, jumps) every frame for precise registration. Lower values reduce accuracy during fast play.
commands.pro_tip: Lock it at 125 — this is the engine’s hard cap and the exact value every pro uses so the server gets your full movement and shot data 125 times per second for pixel-perfect registration.

commands.name: cl_packetdup
commands.short_description: Number of duplicate movement packets sent to the server as backup. On stable fiber connections, 0 saves bandwidth with no loss; 1 provides redundancy during minor packet drops; Can be set from 0-5.
commands.description: Number of duplicate movement packets sent to the server as backup. On stable fiber connections, 0 saves bandwidth with no loss; 1 provides redundancy during minor packet drops; Can be set from 0-5.
commands.pro_tip: Set to 0 on fiber — you save bandwidth and the server still gets every single movement packet perfectly, which is what all pros on stable connections do.

commands.name: cl_timeNudge
commands.short_description: Artificially shifts your client clock relative to the server to compensate for ping. Negative values (–5 to –20) make distant enemies appear slightly ahead in time, improving hitscan (rail) registration on higher-latency connections. Too negative causes choppy enemy animations; 0 is ideal for low ping or LAN.
commands.description: Artificially shifts your client clock relative to the server to compensate for ping. Negative values (–5 to –20) make distant enemies appear slightly ahead in time, improving hitscan (rail) registration on higher-latency connections. Too negative causes choppy enemy animations; 0 is ideal for low ping or LAN.
commands.pro_tip: Use -5 to -10 on 50–100 ping connections — it pulls enemy positions slightly forward in time so your rails connect exactly where you aimed instead of where they were 80ms ago.

commands.name: cg_predictItems
commands.short_description: Predicts item pickups locally for instant sound/visual feedback instead of waiting for server confirmation. On reduces perceived latency when grabbing items; Off ensures 100% server accuracy but feels slightly delayed. Most pros turn OFF to avoid double-pickup sound confusion.
commands.description: Predicts item pickups locally for instant sound/visual feedback instead of waiting for server confirmation. On reduces perceived latency when grabbing items; Off ensures 100% server accuracy but feels slightly delayed. Most pros turn OFF to avoid double-pickup sound confusion.
commands.pro_tip: Keeping it OFF with high fiber connection ensures you actually pick up the item before hearing the sound for confirmation, which today a lot of high fiber connections can support.

commands.name: cg_drawSnapshot
commands.short_description: Developer overlay showing raw server snapshot rate and timing data. Only enable when diagnosing netcode, lag compensation, or packet issues; Off for normal play.
commands.description: Developer overlay showing raw server snapshot rate and timing data. Only enable when diagnosing netcode, lag compensation, or packet issues; Off for normal play.
commands.pro_tip: Only turn it on when debugging — the tiny numbers in the corner let you instantly see whether you need to tweak rate or cl_maxpackets.

commands.name: net_port
commands.short_description: Local UDP port used for game communication. Fixed ports can conflict on shared routers; 0 lets the OS assign a random port, ideal for multiple QL instances or multi-PC households.
commands.description: Local UDP port used for game communication. Fixed ports can conflict on shared routers; 0 lets the OS assign a random port, ideal for multiple QL instances or multi-PC households.
commands.pro_tip: Set to 0 if you ever run two instances of QL or play on the same network as someone else — the OS picks a random port and you never get “port already in use” errors again.

commands.name: net_socksenabled
commands.short_description: Activates SOCKS5 proxy support for connecting through restricted networks (e.g., corporate/university firewalls). Only enable if direct connections are blocked; otherwise keep Off for lowest latency.
commands.description: Activates SOCKS5 proxy support for connecting through restricted networks (e.g., corporate/university firewalls). Only enable if direct connections are blocked; otherwise keep Off for lowest latency.
commands.pro_tip: Only turn this on if you’re stuck behind a corporate or university firewall — otherwise leave it off because every extra hop adds unnecessary ping.

commands.name: net_socksserver
commands.short_description: IP address or hostname of the SOCKS5 proxy server. Only used when SOCKS is enabled. Leave blank unless you are configuring a proxy.
commands.description: IP address or hostname of the SOCKS5 proxy server. Only used when SOCKS is enabled. Leave blank unless you are configuring a proxy.
commands.pro_tip: Enter your proxy IP here only if you’re using a paid SOCKS5 service for ranked play on restricted networks — otherwise leave it completely blank.

commands.name: net_socksport
commands.short_description: Port number for the SOCKS5 proxy server. Standard is 1080; change only if your proxy uses a non-standard port. Only active when SOCKS is enabled.
commands.description: Port number for the SOCKS5 proxy server. Standard is 1080; change only if your proxy uses a non-standard port. Only active when SOCKS is enabled.
commands.pro_tip: Use 1080 for almost every SOCKS5 service — only change this if your proxy provider specifically tells you a different port.

commands.name: net_socksusername
commands.short_description: Username for authenticated SOCKS5 proxies. Leave blank if your proxy does not require authentication.
commands.description: Username for authenticated SOCKS5 proxies. Leave blank if your proxy does not require authentication.
commands.pro_tip: Only fill this if your SOCKS5 provider gives you credentials — most free proxies are anonymous and leave this field blank.

commands.name: net_sockspassword
commands.short_description: Password for authenticated SOCKS5 proxies. Leave blank if your proxy does not require authentication.
commands.description: Password for authenticated SOCKS5 proxies. Leave blank if your proxy does not require authentication.
commands.pro_tip: Only fill this if your SOCKS5 provider gives you credentials — otherwise leave it completely empty to avoid connection errors.

commands.name: s_volume
commands.short_description: Master multiplier for all in-game audio output (weapons, footsteps, pickups, voice). Pros set this low (0.2–0.4) and compensate with external headphone amp or Windows volume to avoid engine clipping/distortion during overlapping loud events (quad + rockets), preserving clear positional cues and reducing ear fatigue.
commands.description: Master multiplier for all in-game audio output (weapons, footsteps, pickups, voice). Pros set this low (0.2–0.4) and compensate with external headphone amp or Windows volume to avoid engine clipping/distortion during overlapping loud events (quad + rockets), preserving clear positional cues and reducing ear fatigue.
commands.pro_tip: Drop to 0.2–0.4 and boost with your headphone amp or Windows volume — this prevents clipping during quad + rocket spam while keeping footsteps crystal clear, which is the exact setup every pro uses.

commands.name: s_musicvolume
commands.short_description: Controls volume of background map music. Competitive players universally set this to 0 because music masks critical positional audio cues such as enemy footsteps, weapon switches, and item pickups.
commands.description: Controls volume of background map music. Competitive players universally set this to 0 because music masks critical positional audio cues such as enemy footsteps, weapon switches, and item pickups.
commands.pro_tip: Always set to 0 — music is the #1 thing that hides enemy footsteps and item pickup sounds, which is why no serious player ever has it enabled.

commands.name: s_doppler
commands.short_description: Enables pitch-shifting 'swoosh' on passing projectiles (rockets, plasma) based on relative speed. Off keeps consistent pitch for accurate distance judgment by ear—competitive standard because doppler can mislead spatial awareness.
commands.description: Enables pitch-shifting 'swoosh' on passing projectiles (rockets, plasma) based on relative speed. Off keeps consistent pitch for accurate distance judgment by ear—competitive standard because doppler can mislead spatial awareness.
commands.pro_tip: Keep it OFF — consistent pitch lets you judge rocket and plasma distance purely by sound volume and tone, which is how pros pre-dodge without even looking.

commands.name: cg_hitBeep
commands.short_description: Changes the pitch of the damage confirmation beep when hitting an opponent. Ascending pitch (2) provides rising tone feedback that instinctively signals sustained Lightning Gun contact and helps maintain beam hold.
commands.description: Changes the pitch of the damage confirmation beep when hitting an opponent. Ascending pitch (2) provides rising tone feedback that instinctively signals sustained Lightning Gun contact and helps maintain beam hold.
commands.pro_tip: Use ascending pitch (2) — the rising tone instinctively tells you when your LG is locked on and dealing max damage, helping you hold the beam longer without looking at the damage numbers.

commands.name: cg_killBeep
commands.short_description: Selects the specific sound that plays when you score a frag. The classic 'Ding' (7) is the most common choice because it is short, distinct, and instantly recognizable during multi-kills.
commands.description: Selects the specific sound that plays when you score a frag. The classic 'Ding' (7) is the most common choice because it is short, distinct, and instantly recognizable during multi-kills.
commands.pro_tip: Stick with the classic Ding (7) — it’s short, unmistakable even in the middle of a multi-kill frenzy, and every pro instantly knows they just secured the frag.

commands.name: s_killBeepVolume
commands.short_description: Independent volume control for the kill confirmation sound. Pros lower this (0.6–0.8) to prevent it from overpowering footsteps or other critical cues during multi-frag sequences.
commands.description: Independent volume control for the kill confirmation sound. Pros lower this (0.6–0.8) to prevent it from overpowering footsteps or other critical cues during multi-frag sequences.
commands.pro_tip: Lower to 0.6–0.8 — the ding stays audible but never drowns out footsteps or the next enemy’s rail charge, which is critical during 3+ kill streaks.

commands.name: cg_announcer
commands.short_description: Selects the voice pack for in-game announcer events ('Excellent', 'Frag', 'Two Minutes Warning', etc.). The classic male voice (1) remains the most common in competitive play for familiarity and clarity.
commands.description: Selects the voice pack for in-game announcer events ('Excellent', 'Frag', 'Two Minutes Warning', etc.). The classic male voice (1) remains the most common in competitive play for familiarity and clarity.
commands.pro_tip: Stick with the classic male voice (1) — it’s the one every veteran instantly recognizes, so you never miss a “Two Minutes Left” call even in the middle of a firefight.

commands.name: s_announcerVolume
commands.short_description: Independent volume control for announcer voice lines ('Excellent', 'Time Warning', etc.). Pros lower this (0.4–0.5) so map announcements don't overpower immediate combat audio like footsteps or weapon switches.
commands.description: Independent volume control for announcer voice lines ('Excellent', 'Time Warning', etc.). Pros lower this (0.4–0.5) so map announcements don't overpower immediate combat audio like footsteps or weapon switches.
commands.pro_tip: Drop to 0.4–0.5 — the announcer stays audible for important calls but never drowns out enemy footsteps or weapon switches during fights.

commands.name: s_voiceVolume
commands.short_description: Volume level for other players' in-game VoIP microphone communication. Pros keep this high (0.8–1.0) for clear team callouts in CTF/TDM while ensuring it doesn't overpower footsteps or hitsounds.
commands.description: Volume level for other players' in-game VoIP microphone communication. Pros keep this high (0.8–1.0) for clear team callouts in CTF/TDM while ensuring it doesn't overpower footsteps or hitsounds.
commands.pro_tip: Set to 0.8–1.0 — clear teammate callouts (“flag low!” or “quad mid!”) are worth every decibel while still leaving room for footsteps and hitsounds.

commands.name: cg_chatBeep
commands.short_description: Plays a short notification sound whenever a player sends a message in global chat. On provides an audible cue for chat activity; Off reduces minor distractions.
commands.description: Plays a short notification sound whenever a player sends a message in global chat. On provides an audible cue for chat activity; Off reduces minor distractions.
commands.pro_tip: Turn it OFF in ranked — the random beeps from global chat are pure distraction when you’re trying to focus on footsteps and positioning.

commands.name: cg_teamChatBeep
commands.short_description: Plays a short notification sound when a teammate sends a message in team chat. On ensures you hear important calls (weapon drops, flags); Off reduces audio clutter in busy games.
commands.description: Plays a short notification sound when a teammate sends a message in team chat. On ensures you hear important calls (weapon drops, flags); Off reduces audio clutter in busy games.
commands.pro_tip: Leave it ON — the beep for teammate “lg drop” or “flag taken” is the fastest way to react without having to read chat during a fight.

commands.name: s_mixahead
commands.short_description: Controls the audio buffer size, essentially determining how far ahead the game engine mixes sounds before they are sent to your speakers or headphones. Lower values (0.08–0.10) minimize delay for near-instant weapon firing and hit feedback on modern systems; higher values prevent crackling on older hardware (and are safe). Note: Lowering this value below 0.08 can disable sounds at all. Modifying it only slightly below default is recommended and to test values
commands.description: Controls the audio buffer size, essentially determining how far ahead the game engine mixes sounds before they are sent to your speakers or headphones. Lower values (0.08–0.10) minimize delay for near-instant weapon firing and hit feedback on modern systems; higher values prevent crackling on older hardware (and are safe). Note: Lowering this value below 0.08 can disable sounds at all. Modifying it only slightly below default is recommended and to test values
commands.pro_tip: 99% of players will be safe with the default 0.14 — but if you test lowering down to 0.8-0.9, the near-zero audio delay makes weapon switch and hit feedback feel instant, which is what you want to achieve real-time play.

available_settings.description: 0.08 - May crackle here or below (Lower is dangerous)
0.10 - High End Sweet Spot (Safe Tweak Recommendation)

commands.name: s_mixPreStep
commands.short_description: Pre-build buffer step time in seconds, used with s_mixahead for smooth audio mixing. Must always stay noticeably smaller than s_mixahead (good margin recommended). This editor only recommends users to test any s_mixahead value higher than or equal to 0.08, so never go below 0.03 here. Default 0.05 is what 99% of stable configs and pros use.
commands.description: Pre-build buffer step time in seconds, used with s_mixahead for smooth audio mixing. Must always stay noticeably smaller than s_mixahead (good margin recommended). This editor only recommends users to test any s_mixahead value higher than or equal to 0.08, so never go below 0.03 here. Default 0.05 is what 99% of stable configs and pros use.
commands.pro_tip: Keep the default 0.05 in almost all cases. If you have lowered your s_mixahead to 0.10 or below, you may need to drop this to 0.03 or 0.04 to ensure the audio buffer stays filled. Always test this in a busy 16-player server; if you hear "stuttering" raise it back up. Crucial: Never let this value get close to your s_mixahead value, or sounds will cut out entirely.

commands.name: com_hunkMegs
commands.short_description: Allocates RAM in megabytes to the core Quake engine for loading maps, models, textures, and other static assets. The original default of 128MB dates back to 2008 and frequently causes crashes or 'hunk overflow' errors when loading large custom Steam Workshop maps or high-res texture packs. In 2026, pros set this to 512MB or higher to ensure stability with modern community content and large-scale servers.
commands.description: Allocates RAM in megabytes to the core Quake engine for loading maps, models, textures, and other static assets. The original default of 128MB dates back to 2008 and frequently causes crashes or 'hunk overflow' errors when loading large custom Steam Workshop maps or high-res texture packs. In 2026, pros set this to 512MB or higher to ensure stability with modern community content and large-scale servers.
commands.pro_tip: Set to 512MB or higher — this single change stops all “hunk overflow” crashes on modern custom maps and high-res texture packs that every pro runs in 2026.

commands.name: com_soundMegs
commands.short_description: Allocates RAM in megabytes for buffering sound samples and audio assets. The default 8MB is sufficient for base game sounds but can cause stuttering or missing effects with high-quality custom sound packs or modified announcer voices. Pros increase to 32MB or more for smooth playback in custom servers.
commands.description: Allocates RAM in megabytes for buffering sound samples and audio assets. The default 8MB is sufficient for base game sounds but can cause stuttering or missing effects with high-quality custom sound packs or modified announcer voices. Pros increase to 32MB or more for smooth playback in custom servers.
commands.pro_tip: Bump to 32MB — custom announcer packs and high-quality sound mods never stutter or drop sounds again, which is why every pro who uses custom audio runs this value.

commands.name: com_zoneMegs
commands.short_description: Allocates RAM in megabytes for dynamic engine data like console variables, scripts, and config parsing. The default 24MB can overflow with very large or complex configs (e.g., custom HUDs or binds), causing crashes or errors. Pros raise to 32MB+ for stability with advanced setups.
commands.description: Allocates RAM in megabytes for dynamic engine data like console variables, scripts, and config parsing. The default 24MB can overflow with very large or complex configs (e.g., custom HUDs or binds), causing crashes or errors. Pros raise to 32MB+ for stability with advanced setups.
commands.pro_tip: Set to 32MB — massive autoexec.cfg files with hundreds of binds and custom HUD scripts never cause zone overflow crashes again.

commands.name: com_idlesleep
commands.short_description: Allows engine idle/sleep when minimized/unfocused (1 saves CPU, risks stutter/disconnects). Off (0) keeps full background activity for seamless alt-tab audio/netgraph. Pros use 0 for stability on Linux/Windows.
commands.description: Allows engine idle/sleep when minimized/unfocused (1 saves CPU, risks stutter/disconnects). Off (0) keeps full background activity for seamless alt-tab audio/netgraph. Pros use 0 for stability on Linux/Windows.
commands.pro_tip: Set to 0 — the game stays fully awake when alt-tabbed so netgraph, audio, and background demos never stutter or disconnect when you quickly switch back.

commands.name: com_introplayed
commands.short_description: Bypasses the introductory Bethesda and id Software splash videos and audio on game launch. Pros enable this to reduce startup time and eliminate unnecessary noise, allowing quicker entry into practice or matches.
commands.description: Bypasses the introductory Bethesda and id Software splash videos and audio on game launch. Pros enable this to reduce startup time and eliminate unnecessary noise, allowing quicker entry into practice or matches.
commands.pro_tip: Set to 1 — the game launches straight to the menu in 2 seconds instead of forcing you to sit through 8 seconds of logos every single time.

commands.name: bot_nochat
commands.short_description: Mutes bot chat/taunts/messages in offline. On eliminates peripheral chat spam for uninterrupted aim/movement practice.
commands.description: Mutes bot chat/taunts/messages in offline. On eliminates peripheral chat spam for uninterrupted aim/movement practice.
commands.pro_tip: Turn it ON — bot spam chat is the single biggest distraction when you’re trying to grind aim or movement drills in offline.

commands.name: bot_challenge
commands.short_description: Activates advanced bot AI: better prediction, aggressive item timing, superhuman dodge. On for high-level offline training.
commands.description: Activates advanced bot AI: better prediction, aggressive item timing, superhuman dodge. On for high-level offline training.
commands.pro_tip: Turn it ON — the “sweaty” bots play like actual ranked opponents with perfect prediction and movement, making your offline practice actually translate to real games.

commands.name: bot_rocketjump
commands.short_description: Permits bots to rocketjump for high items. On replicates human mobility for realistic practice.
commands.description: Permits bots to rocketjump for high items. On replicates human mobility for realistic practice.
commands.pro_tip: Leave it ON — bots rocketjump and grab high items exactly like real players, so your offline movement and timing drills are 100% realistic.

commands.name: g_spskill
commands.short_description: Offline bot difficulty (1 easy-5 nightmare). 4 hard for pro warmup; 5 aimbot for hitscan drills.
commands.description: Offline bot difficulty (1 easy-5 nightmare). 4 hard for pro warmup; 5 aimbot for hitscan drills.
commands.pro_tip: Set to 4 or 5 — these bots move and aim like real ranked opponents, turning your offline warm-up into actual useful training instead of mindless farming.
